items.title: Tracking MiniDionis: CozyCar's New Ride Is Related to Seaduke
items.description: A new campaign attributed to CozyDuke threat actors has been identified, utilizing malware called MiniDionis that appears related to Seaduke. The campaign began on July 7, 2015, targeting government organizations and think-tanks in democratic countries through spear phishing emails containing malicious links or attachments. The attack chain involves multi-stage droppers that deliver decoy media fi
items.ai_summary: The MiniDionis malware campaign, attributed to CozyDuke threat actors, utilizes spear phishing emails with malicious links or attachments to target government organizations and think-tanks in democratic countries. The attack chain involves multi-stage droppers that deliver decoy media files, with the ultimate goal of establishing a foothold in the target network. If exploited, this campaign can lead to unauthorized access to sensitive information and potential data breaches, as seen in the targeting of government organizations. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust email filtering solutions, conduct regular security awareness training for employees, and ensure that all systems are configured to block suspicious attachments and links. Additionally, network traffic monitoring should be implemented to detect and block potential command and control communications.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1104| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: LBIOC-20260071 - The Gentlemens Leak
items.description: The Gentlemen is an active ransomware and extortion operation that emerged publicly in the second half of 2025, rapidly escalating into a high-volume threat actor. The group appears to be a continuation or reorganization of prior ransomware affiliate activity, with reported connections to the Qilin ecosystem and the Russian-speaking actor 'hastalamuerte.' This growth likely reflects existing ranso
items.ai_summary: The Gentlemen ransomware operation, also known as LBIOC-20260071, has been identified as a high-volume threat actor with connections to the Qilin ecosystem and the Russian-speaking actor 'hastalamuerte.' This group utilizes tactics such as stealing or forging authentication certificates (T1649) to gain initial access, and then encrypts data for impact (T1486), leading to significant disruptions for affected organizations. To mitigate this threat, defenders should prioritize implementing robust authentication mechanisms, such as multi-factor authentication, and ensuring that all systems are up-to-date with the latest security patches. Additionally, organizations should consider conducting regular security audits to identify and address potential vulnerabilities. In the event of a ransomware attack, it is essential to have a comprehensive incident response plan in place, including data backups and a communication strategy to minimize the impact of the attack.

    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic

items.title: Threat Actors Leverage Claude Code Leak as Social Engineering Lure to Distribute Malicious Payloads via GitHub
items.description: Cybercriminals are exploiting the recent Claude Code leak incident by using it as a social engineering tactic to deliver malware through GitHub repositories. The attackers have created trojanized versions of the leaked Claude source code, distributing malicious payloads including Vidar stealer version 18.7 and GhostSocks trojan. The campaign demonstrates rapid opportunistic exploitation of high-pr
items.ai_summary: The recent Claude Code leak has been exploited by threat actors as a social engineering tactic to distribute malicious payloads via GitHub repositories. The attackers have created trojanized versions of the leaked Claude source code, which delivers malware such as Vidar stealer version 18.7 and GhostSocks trojan when executed. To mitigate this threat, defenders should implement strict code review processes for any code downloaded from GitHub, ensuring that only trusted repositories are used and verifying the integrity of the code through hashing and digital signatures. Additionally, users should be cautious when interacting with GitHub repositories, avoiding execution of unknown or untrusted code. Real-world impact includes potential data breaches and unauthorized access to sensitive information, emphasizing the need for swift remediation and user education.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[User Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Threat Actor Targets Arabian Gulf Region With PlugX
items.description: In March 2026, a China-nexus threat actor launched a sophisticated campaign targeting countries in the Arabian Gulf region, exploiting renewed Middle East conflict themes within 24 hours of escalation. The attack utilized Arabic-language lures depicting missile strikes and employed a multi-stage infection chain beginning with weaponized ZIP archives containing malicious LNK and CHM files. The camp
items.ai_summary: The China-nexus threat actor launched a campaign targeting countries in the Arabian Gulf region, utilizing Arabic-language lures and a multi-stage infection chain. The attack began with weaponized ZIP archives containing malicious LNK and CHM files, exploiting user execution (T1204) to gain initial access. The threat actor then used multi-stage channels (T1104) to establish a foothold, and archive via utility (T1560.001) to evade detection. To remediate, defenders should implement robust email filtering, ensure all systems are configured to block malicious LNK and CHM files, and monitor for suspicious activity related to data encryption (T1486). Additionally, defenders should prioritize user education and awareness to prevent user execution of malicious files.

    B -->|T1104| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1560.001| D[Persistence]:::tactic

items.title: Tracking an OtterCookie Infostealer Campaign Across npm
items.description: Between April 6-9, 2026, multiple obfuscated malicious npm packages were identified as variants of the OtterCookie infostealer attributed to North Korean threat actors. The campaign employs a two-layer distribution strategy where benign wrapper packages clone legitimate libraries like big.js while pulling malicious dependencies containing the actual payload. Five malicious packages were identified
items.ai_summary: The OtterCookie infostealer campaign, attributed to North Korean threat actors, involves the distribution of malicious npm packages that employ a two-layer strategy to evade detection. The campaign compromises the software supply chain by creating wrapper packages that appear benign but pull in malicious dependencies containing the actual payload, as indicated by the detection of TTPs T1195 (Supply Chain Compromise) and T1027 (Obfuscated Files or Information). To mitigate this threat, developers should inspect their package dependencies carefully, ensuring that all dependencies are legitimate and up-to-date, and consider using tools like npm audit or snyk to identify potential vulnerabilities. Furthermore, developers should prioritize verifying the authenticity of packages like big.js, which were cloned by the malicious actors, and update their dependencies to the latest versions. Specific remediation steps include removing any identified malicious packages and re-verifying the integrity of their project dependencies.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Obfuscation Without Effort: Breaking a GIFTEDCROOK Stealer
items.description: A fresh GIFTEDCROOK stealer variant was identified as part of a UAC-0226 campaign targeting Ukraine. Initial access leverages CVE-2025-6218 and CVE-2025-8088 through a weaponized RAR archive containing a decoy PDF themed around military registry information. The attack chain uses an LNK file to execute obfuscated PowerShell code that decodes and deploys the payload. The stealer employs RC4 encrypt
items.ai_summary: The GIFTEDCROOK stealer variant exploits vulnerabilities in unpatched systems, leveraging initial access through CVE-2025-6218 and CVE-2025-8088 via a malicious RAR archive containing a decoy PDF. This archive is themed around military registry information, targeting Ukraine as part of the UAC-0226 campaign. Upon execution, an LNK file triggers obfuscated PowerShell code, which decodes and deploys the payload, employing RC4 encryption to steal sensitive data. To remediate, defenders should apply patches for CVE-2025-6218 and CVE-2025-8088, and implement PowerShell version 7 or later with constrained language mode, while also monitoring for suspicious LNK files and RC4 encryption usage. Additionally, ensuring up-to-date antivirus software and educating users about suspicious emails and attachments can help prevent initial access.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1560| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1027| D[Defense Evasion]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: ASO RAT: Arabic-Language Android Surveillance Platform Targeting Syria
items.description: ASO RAT is a custom Android Remote Access Trojan featuring comprehensive device compromise capabilities including SMS interception, camera access, GPS tracking, call logging, file exfiltration, and DDoS functionality. Operating from Frankfurt-based infrastructure with connections to Syria, the platform disguises itself as PDF readers and Syrian government applications. Investigation revealed two a
items.ai_summary: The ASO RAT is a custom Android Remote Access Trojan that targets Syrian individuals by disguising itself as PDF readers and Syrian government applications, allowing threat actors to intercept SMS messages, access cameras, track GPS locations, log calls, exfiltrate files, and conduct DDoS attacks. The RAT operates from Frankfurt-based infrastructure and has been linked to Syrian connections, utilizing T1036 masquerading techniques to evade detection. If exploited, this vulnerability could result in significant privacy and security breaches for affected individuals, potentially leading to identity theft, financial loss, or other malicious activities. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust mobile device security measures, including regular software updates, use of reputable antivirus software, and avoidance of suspicious applications, particularly those originating from untrusted sources. Additionally, users should be cautious when installing applications, especially those that request excessive permissions or appear to be disguised as legitimate software.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1036| C[Persistence]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: REFUNDEE: Inside a Shadow Panel Phishing-as-a-Service Operation
items.description: An open directory discovery at refundonex[.]com exposed a complete Phishing-as-a-Service and RAT-as-a-Service platform targeting Spanish and Portuguese-speaking victims. The investigation uncovered 3,788 files including weaponized LNK, VBS, and AES-encrypted PowerShell payloads delivering a remote access trojan. The platform, called Shadow Panel, operates from Bulgarian infrastructure and offers c
items.ai_summary: The REFUNDEE phishing campaign utilizes a Phishing-as-a-Service and RAT-as-a-Service platform, known as Shadow Panel, to target Spanish and Portuguese-speaking victims. The attack vector involves the use of weaponized LNK, VBS, and AES-encrypted PowerShell payloads to deliver a remote access trojan, with T1566 phishing tactics used for initial access. If exploited, victims may experience unauthorized access to their systems, with potential data breaches and ransomware attacks. To remediate, defenders should implement robust email filtering, using specific configuration changes such as blocking emails with suspicious attachments and implementing two-factor authentication. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious PowerShell activity, such as unusual script executions, and update their security software to detect and block the remote access trojan.

items.title: Live C2 Dump Recovering Every Stage of the Kill Chain: CHM Dropper, VBScript Stager, PowerShell Keylogger
items.description: On April 11, 2026, researchers analyzed a CHM file (api_reference.chm) tagged as Kimsuky that initiated a three-stage attack chain. The C2 server at check[.]nid-log[.]com had directory listing enabled, allowing recovery of complete source code for all payload stages: a 6,338-byte VBScript performing system reconnaissance and establishing persistence via scheduled task, a 449-byte VBScript bridge t
items.ai_summary: The analyzed threat involves a CHM file, specifically 'api_reference.chm', which initiates a three-stage attack chain attributed to the Kimsuky threat actor. This attack chain begins with the exploitation of a public-facing application (T1190), followed by the establishment of persistence via scheduled tasks (T1053) using a 6,338-byte VBScript. The script performs system reconnaissance and sets the stage for further malicious activities, including the execution of a PowerShell keylogger (T1059.001) for input capture (T1056) and file and directory discovery (T1083). To remediate, defenders should immediately block access to the C2 server at 'check[.]nid-log[.]com', inspect scheduled tasks for suspicious VBScripts, and implement PowerShell execution policies to restrict unauthorized script execution. Additionally, monitoring for unusual file and directory activities can help in early detection of similar threats.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1053| C[Persistence]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1056| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Adobe Reader 0-day
items.description: On April 7, 2026, a security researcher described an Adobe Reader zero-day vulnerability that has been exploited since at least December 2025. The vulnerability allows threat actors to execute privileged Acrobat APIs via specially crafted malicious PDF files that execute obfuscated JavaScript when opened. Exploitation allows attackers to steal sensitive user and system data and to potentially laun
items.ai_summary: The Adobe Reader 0-day vulnerability, exploited since December 2025, allows threat actors to execute privileged Acrobat APIs via specially crafted malicious PDF files containing obfuscated JavaScript. This vulnerability affects Adobe Reader versions prior to the latest patch release and can be exploited through spearphishing attachments, as indicated by the detected TTP T1566.001. If exploited, this vulnerability can lead to the theft of sensitive user and system data, as well as the potential launch of further malicious activities. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should immediately update Adobe Reader to the latest version, which includes patches for this 0-day exploit, and ensure that all PDF files are scanned for malicious content before opening. Additionally, defenders should be cautious of spearphishing attempts and educate users on how to identify and avoid suspicious emails with malicious attachments.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566.001| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1649| D[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    D -->|T1021| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-43861: mutt before 2.3.2 does not check for '\0' in url_pct_decode....
items.description: mutt before 2.3.2 does not check for '\0' in url_pct_decode.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-43861 is a vulnerability in mutt versions prior to 2.3.2, where the url_pct_decode function fails to check for null bytes, potentially leading to a denial-of-service condition. This vulnerability can be exploited by an attacker sending a crafted email with a malicious URL, causing the mutt client to crash. Real-world impact includes disruption of email services for users relying on mutt, particularly in Linux environments where mutt is commonly used. To remediate, defenders should update mutt to version 2.3.2 or later and ensure that all dependencies are also up-to-date. Additionally, configuring email clients to use alternative URL decoding mechanisms can provide an extra layer of protection.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-43860: mutt before 2.3.2 sometimes truncates the hash_passwd by one byte for IMAP auth_...
items.description: mutt before 2.3.2 sometimes truncates the hash_passwd by one byte for IMAP auth_cram MD5 digest.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-43860 is a vulnerability in mutt versions prior to 2.3.2, where the hash_passwd is sometimes truncated by one byte during IMAP auth_cram MD5 digest authentication. This vulnerability can lead to authentication bypass, allowing an attacker to access email accounts without the correct password. The real-world impact of this vulnerability is that an attacker could exploit it to gain unauthorized access to email accounts, potentially leading to sensitive information disclosure. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should update mutt to version 2.3.2 or later and ensure that all IMAP connections use secure authentication mechanisms, such as OAuth or TLS-encrypted connections. Additionally, defenders should monitor their email servers for suspicious login activity and implement rate limiting on authentication attempts to prevent brute-force attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1110| B[Brute Force]:::tactic
    B -->|T1550| C[Session Hijacking]:::tactic
    C -->|T1021| D[Remote Services]:::tactic
    D -->|T1582| E[Execution with Unnecessary Privileges]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-43859: mutt before 2.3.2 sometimes uses strfcpy instead of memcpy for the IMAP auth_cra...
items.description: mutt before 2.3.2 sometimes uses strfcpy instead of memcpy for the IMAP auth_cram MD5 digest.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-43859 is a medium-severity vulnerability in mutt before 2.3.2, where the IMAP auth_cram MD5 digest is sometimes copied using the insecure strfcpy function instead of memcpy. This allows an attacker to potentially exploit the vulnerability by crafting a malicious IMAP response, which could lead to a buffer overflow and arbitrary code execution. The affected systems include mutt versions prior to 2.3.2, and the attack vector is a malicious IMAP server. To remediate this issue, users should upgrade to mutt 2.3.2 or later, and ensure that the IMAP server is configured to use a secure authentication mechanism. Additionally, users should consider implementing additional security measures, such as IMAP server authentication and authorization, to prevent unauthorized access.

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-29200: A critical IDOR vulnerability has been discovered in Comet Backup affecting all ...
items.description: A critical IDOR vulnerability has been discovered in Comet Backup affecting all versions from 20.11.0 to 26.1.1 and 26.2.1. The vulnerability allows a tenant administrator to impersonate any end-user account of other tenants on the same server via a vulnerable API call.
items.ai_summary: A critical IDOR vulnerability (CVE-2026-29200) has been discovered in Comet Backup affecting all versions from 20.11.0 to 26.1.1 and 26.2.1. This vulnerability allows a tenant administrator to impersonate any end-user account of other tenants on the same server via a vulnerable API call. If exploited, an attacker can gain unauthorized access to sensitive data and potentially take control of other tenants' accounts. To remediate this issue, administrators should immediately update to version 26.2.2 or later, and ensure that the 'allow_impersonation' setting is disabled in the API configuration. Additionally, it is recommended to monitor API calls for suspicious activity and implement additional access controls to prevent similar attacks in the future.

items.title: CVE-2026-29199: phpBB before 3.3.16 is vulnerable to Host Header Injection that can lead to pass...
items.description: phpBB before 3.3.16 is vulnerable to Host Header Injection that can lead to password rest link poisoning. When force_server_vars is disabled, the servers hostname may be extracted from the HTTP Host header which is used to generate the password reset link URL. An attacker who can manipulate the Host header (e.g. through misconfigured host setup or missing header validation by the webserver) can ca
items.ai_summary: The phpBB before 3.3.16 vulnerability (CVE-2026-29199) allows an attacker to manipulate the Host header, potentially leading to password reset link poisoning. This occurs when force_server_vars is disabled, allowing the server's hostname to be extracted from the HTTP Host header. An attacker can exploit this by manipulating the Host header, which can be done through misconfigured host setups or missing header validation by the webserver. To remediate, update to phpBB 3.3.16 or later, and ensure that the webserver is configured to validate and sanitize the Host header. Additionally, consider implementing additional security measures such as HTTP Host header validation and input validation for user-controlled data.

items.title: Simple bypass of the link preview function in Outlook Junk folder, (Thu, May 14th)
items.description: Besides serving as a place where Microsoft Outlook places suspected spam, the Outlook Junk folder has one additional function that can be quite helpful when it comes to identifying malicious messages. Any e-mail placed in this folder is stripped of all formatting, and destinations of all links included in the message become visible to the user, as you can see in the following images which show the same e-mail when it is placed in the inbox, and when it is placed in the Junk folder.

items.description: The Nitrogen ransomware group claims to have hacked the company’s systems, stealing 8TB of data, including confidential documents. The post Foxconn Confirms North American Factories Hit by Cyberattack appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Windows BitLocker zero-day gives access to protected drives, PoC released
items.description: A cybersecurity researcher has published proof-of-concept (PoC) exploits for two unpatched Microsoft Windows vulnerabilities named YellowKey and GreenPlasma, which are a BitLocker bypass and a privilege-escalation flaw. [...]

items.title: Microsoft, Palo Alto Networks Find Many Vulnerabilities by Using AI on Their Own Code
items.description: Microsoft’s MDASH discovered 16 of the Patch Tuesday vulnerabilities, and Palo Alto used Mythos to find dozens of flaws. The post Microsoft, Palo Alto Networks Find Many Vulnerabilities by Using AI on Their Own Code appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Webinar tomorrow: Why security alone won't stop modern attacks
items.description: Tomorrow's webinar examines why prevention alone is no longer enough against modern cyberattacks. The session explores how organizations combine security, backups, and recovery planning to improve cyber resilience after attacks. [...]

items.title: Microsoft fixes BitLocker recovery issue only for Windows 11 users
items.description: Microsoft has addressed a known issue causing some Windows 11 systems to boot into BitLocker recovery after installing the April 2026 Windows security updates. [...]

items.title: Sweet Security Launches Agentic AI Red Teaming to Counter ‘Mythos Moment’
items.description: New “Sweet Attack” platform uses runtime intelligence and continuous agentic red teaming to identify exploitable attack chains human teams may miss. The post Sweet Security Launches Agentic AI Red Teaming to Counter ‘Mythos Moment’ appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Microsoft fixes Windows Autopatch bug installing restricted drivers
items.description: Microsoft has fixed a Windows Autopatch bug that caused driver updates restricted by administrative policies to be deployed on some Autopatch-managed Windows devices in the European Union. [...]

items.title: Microsoft's MDASH AI System Finds 16 Windows Flaws Fixed in Patch Tuesday
items.description: Microsoft has unveiled a new multi-model artificial intelligence (AI)-driven system called MDASH to facilitate vulnerability discovery and remediation at scale, adding that it's being tested by some customers as part of a limited private preview. MDASH, short for multi-model agentic scanning harness, is designed as a model-agnostic system that uses bespoke AI agents for different vulnerability

items.title: Webinar Today: ROI for Cyber-Physical Security Programs
items.description: This webinar will help OT security teams and asset owners stop being cost centers and start being resilience drivers. The post Webinar Today: ROI for Cyber-Physical Security Programs appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Azerbaijani Energy Firm Hit by Repeated Microsoft Exchange Exploitation
items.description: A threat actor with affiliations to China has been linked to a "multi-wave intrusion" targeting an unnamed Azerbaijani oil and gas company between late December 2025 and late February 2026, marking an expansion of its targeting. The activity has been attributed by Bitdefender with moderate-to-high confidence to a hacking group known as FamousSparrow (aka UAT-9244), which shares some level of

items.title: China's 'FamousSparrow' APT Nests in South Caucasus Energy Firm
items.description: The cyberthreat group targets an Azerbaijani oil and gas firm with repeated attacks, as the China-linked actors extend targeting beyond hospitality, telecom, and government sectors.

items.title: LatAm Vibe Hackers Generate Custom Hacking Tools on the Fly
items.description: In the latest evolution of automated cyberattacks, two threat campaigns heavily leveraged AI agents to support attacks against entities in Mexico and Brazil.

items.title: UK moves to shield security researchers in cybercrime law overhaul
items.description: The proposed reforms, outlined in briefing documents published alongside the King’s Speech opening a new parliamentary session, would update the Computer Misuse Act 1990 as part of a broader national security package focused on cybercrime and digital threats.

items.title: Microsoft on pace to break annual vulnerability record as AI-driven patch wave takes hold
items.description: Five months into 2026, Microsoft has already patched more than 500 vulnerabilities — although the exact monthly count varies depending on whether analysts include Edge, Chromium and fixes shipped earlier in the month.

items.title: Foxconn confirms cyberattack claimed by Nitrogen ransomware gang
items.description: Foxconn, the world's largest electronics manufacturer, says some of its North American factories are now working to resume normal operations after a cyberattack. [...]

items.title: 73 Seconds to Breach, 24 Hours to Patch: The Case for Autonomous Validation
items.description: Attackers can compromise systems in minutes while patching and response still take hours or days. Picus Security breaks down why autonomous validation is becoming critical for modern defense strategies. [...]

items.title: Government to Scrutinize Instructure Over Canvas Disruption, Data Breach
items.description: The Committee on Homeland Security has requested to be briefed on the incident and Instructure’s remediation steps. The post Government to Scrutinize Instructure Over Canvas Disruption, Data Breach appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Microsoft says some users can't install Office on Windows 365 devices
items.description: Microsoft says some customers are experiencing issues downloading and installing Office on their Windows 365 devices. [...]

items.title: [Webinar] How Modern Attack Paths Cross Code, Pipelines, and Cloud
items.description: TL;DR: Stop chasing thousands of "toast" alerts. Join experts from Wiz to learn how hackers connect tiny flaws to build a "Lethal Chain" to your data—and how to break it. Register for the Strategic Briefing Here. Most security tools work like a smoke alarm that goes off every time you burn a piece of toast. You get so many alerts that you eventually start to ignore them. The real danger? While

items.title: Most Remediation Programs Never Confirm the Fix Actually Worked
items.description: Security teams have never had better visibility into their environments and never been worse at confirming what they fix stays fixed. Mandiant's M-Trends 2026 report puts the mean time to exploit at an estimated negative seven days. The Verizon 2025 DBIR puts median time to remediate edge device vulnerabilities at 32 days. These numbers have understandably driven the industry toward a clear

items.title: 716,000 Impacted by OpenLoop Health Data Breach
items.description: The telehealth platform was hacked in January, and users’ personal information was exfiltrated from its systems. The post 716,000 Impacted by OpenLoop Health Data Breach appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Microsoft Patches 138 Vulnerabilities, Including DNS and Netlogon RCE Flaws
items.description: Microsoft on Tuesday released patches for 138 security vulnerabilities spanning its product portfolio, although none of them have been listed as publicly known or under active attack. Of the 138 flaws, 30 are rated Critical, 104 are rated Important, three are rated Moderate, and one is rated Low in severity. As many as 61 vulnerabilities are classified as privilege escalation bugs, followed by

items.title: Microsoft Patches Critical Zero-Click Outlook Vulnerability Threatening Enterprises
items.description: CVE-2026-40361 is similar to a vulnerability found a decade ago, BadWinmail, which at the time was dubbed an “enterprise killer”. The post Microsoft Patches Critical Zero-Click Outlook Vulnerability Threatening Enterprises appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Fortinet, Ivanti Patch Critical Vulnerabilities
items.description: Successful exploitation of these flaws could lead to arbitrary code execution and information disclosure. The post Fortinet, Ivanti Patch Critical Vulnerabilities appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Chipmaker Patch Tuesday: Intel and AMD Patch 70 Vulnerabilities
items.description: The two chip giants have published over two dozen advisories describing recently identified security defects. The post Chipmaker Patch Tuesday: Intel and AMD Patch 70 Vulnerabilities appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: GemStuffer Abuses 150+ RubyGems to Exfiltrate Scraped U.K. Council Portal Data
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers are calling attention to a new campaign dubbed GemStuffer that has targeted the RubyGems repository with more than 150 gems that use the registry as a data exfiltration channel rather than for malware distribution. "The packages do not appear designed for mass developer compromise," Socket said. "Many have little or no download activity, and the payloads are repetitive,

items.title: Hundreds of Malicious Packages Force RubyGems to Suspend Registrations
items.description: More than 500 packages were pushed during the attack, but the target appears to have been RubyGems itself rather than users. The post Hundreds of Malicious Packages Force RubyGems to Suspend Registrations appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Android Adds Intrusion Logging for Sophisticated Spyware Forensics
items.description: Google on Tuesday unveiled a new opt-in Android feature called Intrusion Logging for storing forensic logs to better analyze sophisticated spyware attacks. Intrusion Logging, available as part of Advanced Protection Mode, enables "persistent and privacy-preserving forensics logging to allow for investigation of devices in the event of a suspected compromise," the company said. The feature, it

items.title: [GUEST DIARY] Tearing apart website fraud to see how it works., (Wed, May 13th)
items.description: &&#x23;x26;&#x23;x5b;This is a Guest Diary by Joshua Nikolson, an ISC Intern and part of the SANS.edu Bachelor&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s degree in Applied Cybersecurity (BACS) program.]

items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Proxying the Unproxyable? Sending EXE traffic to a Proxy, (Wed, May 13th)
items.description: .. if â€œunproxyableâ€ is a word that is ..

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: It's Patch Tuesday for Microsoft & Not a Zero-Day In Sight
items.description: It's the first time in two years with no zero-days. But with 137 flaws to patch, including nine critical ones, admins still have plenty of work to do.

items.title: Foxconn confirms cyberattack impacting North American factories
items.description: A spokesperson for the company confirmed the incident but declined to provide specifics on how many factories in North America were impacted. Foxconn has factories in Wisconsin, Ohio, Texas, Virginia, Indiana and several across Mexico.

items.title: Microsoft May 2026 Patch Tuesday, (Tue, May 12th)
items.description: Today&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s Microsoft patch Tuesday fixes 137 different vulnerabilities. In addition, the update addresses 137 Chromium-related issues affecting Microsoft Edge.

items.title: New Exim BDAT Vulnerability Exposes GnuTLS Builds to Potential Code Execution
items.description: Exim has released security updates to address a severe security issue affecting certain configurations that could enable memory corruption and potential code execution. Exim is an open-source Mail Transfer Agent (MTA) designed for Unix-like systems to receive, route, and deliver email. The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-45185 (CVSS score: 9.8), aka Dead.Letter, has been described as a

items.title: RubyGems Suspends New Signups After Hundreds of Malicious Packages Are Uploaded
items.description: RubyGems, the standard package manager for the Ruby programming language, has temporarily paused account sign ups following what has been described as a "major malicious attack." "We're dealing with a major malicious attack on RubyGems right now," Maciej Mensfeld, senior product manager for software supply chain security at Mend.io, said in a post on X. "Signups are paused for the time being.

items.title: Hugging Face Packages Weaponized With a Single File Tweak
items.description: A tokenizer library file present in Hugging Face AI models can be manipulated to hijack the model's outputs and exfiltrate data.

items.title: New TrickMo Variant Uses TON C2 and SOCKS5 to Create Android Network Pivots
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have flagged a new version of the TrickMo Android banking trojan that uses The Open Network (TON) for command-and-control (C2). The new variant, observed by ThreatFabric between January and February 2026, has been observed actively targeting banking and cryptocurrency wallet users in France, Italy, and Austria. "TrickMo relies on a runtime-loaded APK  (dex.module),

items.title: 20 Leaders Who Built the CISO Era: 2 Decades of Change
items.description: As part of Dark Reading's 20th anniversary special coverage, we profile the CISOs, founders, researchers, criminals, and policymakers who rewrote the enterprise risk playbook.

items.title: Worm Redux: Fresh Mini Shai-Hulud Infections Bite Supply Chain
items.description: Hundreds of npm packages infected by the self-propagating, credential-stealing worm from TeamPCP are related to the open source TanStack ecosystem.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Tuesday, May 12th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9928, (Tue, May 12th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Apple Patches Everything, (Mon, May 11th)
items.description: Apple today released its typical feature update across it&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s operating systems (iOS, iPadOS, macOS, tvOS, watchOS, vision OS). With this update, Apple patched 84 different vulnerabilities. Updates are available for the "26" series of operating systems, as well as for the previous "18" version of iOS/iPadOS, and two versions back for macOS (version 14 and 15).

items.title: Why we use CAPTCHAs, (Mon, May 11th)
items.description: A few months ago, I implemented Cloudflare&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s Turnstile CAPTCHA on some pages. The reason for implementing these CAPTCHAs is obvious: Bots make up a large percentage of traffic and affect site performance.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Monday, May 11th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9926, (Mon, May 11th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Canvas Breach Disrupts Schools & Colleges Nationwide
items.description: An ongoing data extortion attack targeting the widely-used education technology platform Canvas disrupted classes and coursework at school districts and universities across the United States today, after a cybercrime group defaced the service's login page with a ransom demand that threatened to leak data from 275 million students and faculty across nearly 9,000 educational institutions.

items.title: CISA Unveils New Initiative to Fortify America’s Critical Infrastructure
CISA, US and International Partners Release Guide to Secure Adoption of Agentic AI
Anti-DDoS Firm Heaped Attacks on Brazilian ISPs
items.description: A Brazilian tech firm that specializes in protecting networks from distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks has been enabling a botnet responsible for an extended campaign of massive DDoS attacks against other network operators in Brazil, KrebsOnSecurity has learned. The firm's chief executive says the malicious activity resulted from a security breach and was likely the work of a competitor trying to tarnish his company's public image.

items.title: CISA and U.S. Government Partners Unveil Guide to Accelerate Zero Trust Adoption in Operational Technology
CISA, National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) UK, and Global Partners Issue Advisory on Chinese Government-Linked Covert Cyber Networks
CISA Warns of FIRESTARTER Malware Targeting Cisco ASA including Firepower and Secure Firewall Products
‘Scattered Spider’ Member ‘Tylerb’ Pleads Guilty
items.description: A 24-year-old British national and senior member of the cybercrime group "Scattered Spider" has pleaded guilty to wire fraud conspiracy and aggravated identity theft. Tyler Robert Buchanan admitted his role in a series of text-message phishing attacks in the summer of 2022 that allowed the group to hack into at least a dozen major technology companies and steal tens of millions of dollars worth of cryptocurrency from investors.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, April 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft today pushed software updates to fix a staggering 167 security vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and related software, including a SharePoint Server zero-day and a publicly disclosed weakness in Windows Defender dubbed "BlueHammer." Separately, Google Chrome fixed its fourth zero-day of 2026, and an emergency update for Adobe Reader nixes an actively exploited flaw that can lead to remote code execution.

items.title: Russia Hacked Routers to Steal Microsoft Office Tokens
items.description: Hackers linked to Russia's military intelligence units are using known flaws in older Internet routers to mass harvest authentication tokens from Microsoft Office users, security experts warned today. The spying campaign allowed state-backed Russian hackers to quietly siphon authentication tokens from users on more than 18,000 networks without deploying any malicious software or code.

items.title: Germany Doxes “UNKN,” Head of RU Ransomware Gangs REvil, GandCrab
items.description: An elusive hacker who went by the handle "UNKN" and ran the early Russian ransomware groups GandCrab and REvil now has a name and a face. Authorities in Germany say 31-year-old Russian Daniil Maksimovich Shchukin headed both cybercrime gangs and helped carry out at least 130 acts of computer sabotage and extortion against victims across the country between 2019 and 2021.

items.title: ‘CanisterWorm’ Springs Wiper Attack Targeting Iran
items.description: A financially motivated data theft and extortion group is attempting to inject itself into the Iran war, unleashing a worm that spreads through poorly secured cloud services and wipes data on infected systems that use Iran's time zone or have Farsi set as the default language.

items.title: Feds Disrupt IoT Botnets Behind Huge DDoS Attacks
items.description: The U.S. Justice Department joined authorities in Canada and Germany in dismantling the online infrastructure behind four highly disruptive botnets that compromised more than three million hacked Internet of Things (IoT) devices, such as routers and web cameras. The feds say the four botnets -- named Aisuru, Kimwolf, JackSkid and Mossad -- are responsible for a series of recent record-smashing distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks capable of knocking nearly any target offline.

items.title: Iran-Backed Hackers Claim Wiper Attack on Medtech Firm Stryker
items.description: A hacktivist group with links to Iran's intelligence agencies is claiming responsibility for a data-wiping attack against Stryker, a global medical technology company based in Michigan. News reports out of Ireland, Stryker's largest hub outside of the United States, said the company sent home more than 5,000 workers there today. Meanwhile, a voicemail message at Stryker's main U.S. headquarters says the company is currently experiencing a building emergency.

items.title: CISA Issues Updated RESURGE Malware Analysis Highlighting a Stealthy but Active Threat
Immediate Action Required: CISA Issues Emergency Directive to Secure Cisco SD-WAN Systems
CISA Announces New Town Halls to Engage with Stakeholders on Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure
CISA’s 2025 Year in Review: Driving Security and Resilience Across Critical Infrastructure
CISA Releases Guide to Help Critical Infrastructure Users Adopt More Secure Communication
Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
